verses.text: Damascus shall cease to be a city and become a ruinous heap, and Ephraim's fortress shall fail with her. The allied kingdoms fall together. Those who lean on one another against God perish together.
Damascus shall cease to be a city and become a ruinous heap, and Ephraim's fortress shall fail with her. The allied kingdoms fall together. Those who lean on one another against God perish together.
Damascus shall cease to be a city and become a ruinous heap, and Ephraim's fortress shall fail with her. The allied kingdoms fall together. Those who lean on one another against God perish together.
The glory of Jacob shall be made thin, yet a gleaning shall be left, as a few berries on the topmost bough. Judgment spares a remnant. God in wrath remembers mercy, leaving a few for Himself.
The glory of Jacob shall be made thin, yet a gleaning shall be left, as a few berries on the topmost bough. Judgment spares a remnant. God in wrath remembers mercy, leaving a few for Himself.
The glory of Jacob shall be made thin, yet a gleaning shall be left, as a few berries on the topmost bough. Judgment spares a remnant. God in wrath remembers mercy, leaving a few for Himself.

In that day a man shall look to his Maker and the Holy One of Israel, and not to the altars his own fingers made. Affliction turns the heart back to God. Stripped of idols, men learn to seek their true Maker.

In that day a man shall look to his Maker and the Holy One of Israel, and not to the altars his own fingers made. Affliction turns the heart back to God. Stripped of idols, men learn to seek their true Maker.

In that day a man shall look to his Maker and the Holy One of Israel, and not to the altars his own fingers made. Affliction turns the heart back to God. Stripped of idols, men learn to seek their true Maker.
Because they forgot 'the God of their salvation' and trusted strange plants, their harvest shall be a heap of grief. Forgetting God brings sorrow. What is planted apart from Him yields only loss.
Because they forgot 'the God of their salvation' and trusted strange plants, their harvest shall be a heap of grief. Forgetting God brings sorrow. What is planted apart from Him yields only loss.

The roar of many nations is like the sea, but God rebukes them and they flee like chaff before the wind; 'at eveningtide trouble; and before the morning he is not.' Their menace is brief. The God who stills the sea scatters the foes of His people overnight.
